title: EXPOSÉ XIV OPEN PROBLEMS IN INTERSECTION THEORY

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XIV OPEN PROBLEMS IN INTERSECTION THEORY

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XIV
OPEN PROBLEMS IN INTERSECTION THEORYby A. Grothendieck

In the present exposé we review some of the problems connected with the questions encountered in this Seminar, and which would deserve to be studied. We leave aside the problems connected with the local intersection theory à la Serre [14], which we have not had to use in the Seminar, and refer in particular to loc. cit. for the two fundamental conjectures which remain open in this direction (loc. cit. Chap V § 4). We also pass over in silence the problems, quite important for arithmetic applications, connected with the structure of the groups of cycle classes, notably on a smooth projective algebraic scheme; for the discussion of these problems we refer to S. KLEIMAN, Algebraic cycles and the Weil conjectures in Dix exposés sur la cohomologie des schémas, (Masson-North Holland Publishing Cie). In n° 4 of the present exposé, we also give some complements to the Seminar, concerning in particular the Chow ring, which had not found their place in the preceding exposés.
1. Operations \(\lambda^i\) in the derived category \(D(X)\) (*)

Let \(X\) be a locally ringed topos, and let \(K'(X)\) be the ring of classes of perfect complexes on \(X\) (Exp. IV). One would like to define on \(K'(X)\) a \(\lambda\)-ring structure (Exp. V), functorial in \(X\), such that the natural homomorphism
\[K'_{\mathrm{naïf}}(X)\longrightarrow K'(X)\]

(where the first member is the ring of classes of locally free Modules on \(X\), equipped with the \(\lambda\)-structure defined in Exp. V) is a \(\lambda\)-homomorphism. To this end, one would need to define functors (non-additive in general, of "exterior power")
\[\mathop{\lambda}^{i}:D^{-}(X)\longrightarrow D^{-}(X),\]

transforming pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect) complexes into pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect) complexes, and having the formal properties needed, with respect to exact triangles, to define a \(\lambda\)-structure on \(K'(X)\) by the formula
\[(1.1)\qquad \lambda^i(\operatorname{cl}'(L.))=\operatorname{cl}'(\mathop{\lambda}^{i}L.).\]
When \(X\) is of characteristic zero, i.e. its structure sheaf is a sheaf of \(\mathbb Q\)-algebras, the problem is solved simply by taking
\[(1.2)\qquad \mathop{\lambda}^{i}L.=(\mathop{\otimes}^{i}L.)^{\mathrm{alt}}\]

where one regards the i-th tensor power (in the sense of derived categories) of \(L.\) as being an object of \(D^{-}(\mathcal O_i)\), \(\mathcal O_i\) being the algebra of the symmetric group \(\mathfrak S_i\) with coefficients in \(\mathcal O_X\), and where the exponent alt denotes the passage to the "alternating part under the action of \(\mathfrak S_i\)" (an operation which makes sense thanks to the characteristic zero hypothesis). A formula of this type was already used in [6] (cited hereafter [RRR]), for the first proof of the Riemann-Roch theorem.

Without a hypothesis on the characteristic, when \(L.\) is zero in degrees \(>0\), then by Dold-Puppe [6] \(L.\) is canonically homotopic to the complex associated with a semi-simplicial Module \(L.'\) canonically associated with \(L.\), and a fairly natural candidate for \(\mathop{\lambda}^{i}L.\) (for \(L.\) flat, say) would be the complex associated with the semi-simplicial Module \(\mathop{\lambda}^{i}L.'\) (whose component of degree \(n\) is \(\mathop{\lambda}^{i}L'_n\)). But to extend the definition to complexes of \(D^{-}(X)\) which are no longer assumed zero in degrees \(>0\), one would have to study the behaviour of the preceding operation with respect to the translation operation \(L.\mapsto L.[1]\) (or suspension, in the terminology of [6]); unfortunately, it does not seem that one has an isomorphism \(\mathop{\lambda}^{i}(L.[1])\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\mathop{\lambda}^{i}(L.)[i]\) which would allow one to make the desired extension in a trivial way.

pages.title: 2. The Riemann-Roch formula without projective hypotheses

blocks.html: 2. The Riemann-Roch formula without projective hypotheses

2.1. Once in possession of a natural \(\lambda\)-structure on the \(K'(X)\), it is possible to formulate the Riemann-Roch theorem, in the form envisaged in Exp. VIII, for any proper complete intersection morphism \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\), (and even, more generally, in the case of a relative scheme \(X\) over a locally ringed topos \(Y\), \(X\) being again subject to the corresponding relative conditions). The Todd class of \(f\) is defined by means of the class in \(K'(X)\) of the relative cotangent complex \(T_f\) for \(f\), constructed in Exp. 0 4.4, which complex is perfect here thanks to the local hypothesis made on \(f\). It seems clear that the proof of the Riemann-Roch formula in this general case will require the introduction of essentially new ideas, and it is possible that these same ideas could give the key

to a proof of the analogous formula in the complex analytic case (or the rigid-analytic one!). Recall that in the complex analytic case, only the case of a locally free sheaf \(F\) on a compact non-singular variety \(X\), over a one-point \(Y\), is known at present (thanks to the Atiyah-Singer index formula), the case where \(F\) is only assumed coherent seeming still to escape the techniques known up to now.

2.2. As an essential ingredient of the Riemann-Roch formula, let us also recall for the record the finiteness problem raised in Exp. III: if \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) is a proper morphism, and \(L.\) a complex on \(X\) which is pseudo-coherent relative to \(Y\), then is \(Rf_*(L.)\) pseudo-coherent? A general proof of this conjecture will probably bring into play algebraic ideas which will make it possible to give a proof of Grauert's finiteness "theorem" [7] under conditions notably more general than in loc. cit., by placing oneself from the outset over base spaces (or topoi) topologically ringed in \(\mathbb C\)-algebras \(Y\) more general than analytic spaces (encompassing the case where \(Y\) would be a locally compact space with the sheaf of continuous complex functions, or a \(C^m\) manifold with the sheaf of complex \(C^m\) functions, or a real analytic space, etc).

2.3. Let us mention finally that there would be reason to replace, in these questions, the properness hypothesis on the morphism \(f\) by the hypothesis that the cohomology sheaves \(H^i(L.)\) of the complexes envisaged on \(X\) have their groups proper over \(Y\).

pages.title: 3. Riemann-Roch formula "without denominators" for an immersion

blocks.html: 3. Riemann-Roch formula "without denominators" for an immersion

3.1. Let \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) be a regular closed immersion (Exp. VII), with conormal sheaf \(N\). Suppose for simplicity that \(X\) admits an ample invertible sheaf. The Riemann-Roch formula for \(y\in K'(Y)\):
\[\operatorname{ch}_X(f_*(y))=f_*\bigl(\operatorname{ch}_Y(y)\operatorname{Todd}(-\check N)\bigr),\]
together with the general formula (Exp. VII):
\[f_*(y')f_*(y'')=f_*\bigl(y'y''c_d(\check N)\bigr),\qquad y',y''\in\operatorname{Gr}'(Y)_{\mathbb Q},\]
(assuming \(f\) of codimension \(d\)), makes it possible to express the Chern classes of \(f_*(y)\) in \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\) in the form
\[(3.1)\qquad c_i(f_*(y))=f_*\bigl(P_i((c_\alpha(y)),(c_\beta(\check N)))\bigr),\]

where \(P_i\) is a universal polynomial in the \(c_\alpha(y)\), \(c_\beta(N)\), a polynomial which a priori has rational coefficients. In fact, one observes [RRR] that these polynomials have integer coefficients. This raises the question of the validity of a Riemann-Roch formula "without denominators". In fact, such a formula for \(c_i(f_*(y))\) cannot in general be sought in \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) itself, since the homomorphism \(f_*:K'(Y)\longrightarrow K'(X)\) is not itself compatible with the \(\lambda\)-filtrations modulo a shift by \(d\) (4.6), but is so only up to torsion; so that it is not possible to define a homomorphism \(f_*:\operatorname{Gr}'(Y)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) giving the natural homomorphism \(f_*:\operatorname{Gr}'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\), and making it possible to give a meaning to formula (3.1) as a formula in \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\), and not only in \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)_{\mathbb Q}\). But when \(X\) is assumed regular, hence \(K'(X)\mathbin{\widetilde\longrightarrow}K(X)\) (IV 2.5), one can equip \(K'(X)\) with the topological filtration by the codimension of supports, inherited from the corresponding filtration of \(K_*(X)\). It is plausible that this filtration is compatible with the ring structure, a fact which can be proved at least [RRR] when \(X\) is smooth over a field \(k\), using the theory of the Chow ring [4]. When this is so, the associated graded object
\[\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\]
is a graded ring, and assuming \(Y\) also regular, we have done what was needed in order to have a natural homomorphism of degree \(d\) of graded groups
\[f_*=f_*^{\mathrm{top}}:\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(Y)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X).\]

As the \(\lambda\)-filtration of \(K'(X)\) is finer than the topological filtration by codimension just considered (X 1.3.3), we find a canonical homomorphism of graded groups (and even of graded rings)
\[\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X),\]

which is moreover an isomorphism mod torsion (Exp. VIII). This makes it possible, as in [RRR], to consider a theory of Chern classes on \(X\) with values in \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\), and likewise on \(Y\), and thus to give a meaning to formula (3.1) as a formula in \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\). In this form, this formula is proved in [RRR] when \(X\) is of characteristic zero, at least if \(X\) is smooth over a field (a hypothesis serving only to ensure that the topological filtration of \(K'(X)\) is compatible with the ring structure). The characteristic zero hypothesis is used essen-

tially in order to have at one's disposal formulas of the type (1.1), (1.2), and essentially the same proof should apply without any hypothesis on the characteristic, once the question raised in no. 1 is resolved in the affirmative.
3.2. When \(X\) is smooth over a field, we also have at our disposal the Chow ring
\[A(X),\]

and a theory of Chern classes on \(X\) with values in \(A(X)\) [9], finer than the theory with values in \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) which we have just introduced, — these latter Chern classes being images of the Chow-style Chern classes under the canonical surjective homomorphism (cf. 4.1)
\[\phi:A(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X).\]

This homomorphism is an isomorphism mod. torsion, but not an isomorphism in general (4.7). This said, if \(X\) and \(Y\) are smooth, the inclusion \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) again defines a homomorphism of degree \(d\) of graded groups
\[f_*:A(Y)\longrightarrow A(X),\]

which makes it possible to give a meaning to formula (3.1) as a formula in \(A^i(X)\)(*). This formula moreover reduces to \(0=0\) for \(i<d\), and is therefore of interest only for \(i\geq d\). Even for \(i=d=2\) this formula is not proved at present (cf. 4.3), nor is the less precise formula deduced from it by replacing rational equivalence of algebraic cycles by algebraic equivalence. Contrary to the analogous formula in \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\), there does not seem to be any very convincing reason to suppose that the formula without denominators should be true in \(A(X)\). A good way of testing it, given our almost total ignorance of the nature of the groups \(A^i(X)\) for \(i\geq2\), seems to be to test the formulas that can be deduced from it in \(A^1(Z)=\operatorname{Pic}(Z)\), for example by transforming both sides of (3.1) by
\[g_*:A(X)\longrightarrow A(Z),\]
where \(g:X\longrightarrow Z\) is a proper morphism into a smooth quasi-projective \(Z\) of dimension \(\dim X-i+1\).
3.3. Finally, to give a meaning to (3.1), one can also use the cohomological theory of Chern classes (SGA 5 VIII), whenever one has Gysin homomorphisms at one's disposal
\[f_*:H^p(Y,\mu_n^{\otimes i})\longrightarrow H^{p+2d}(X,\mu_n^{\otimes(i+d)})\]
(cf. no. 6), which is the case at least if \(Y\) and \(X\) are both smooth over a scheme \(S\) (thanks to the relative cohomological purity theorem

SGA 4 XVI 3.7), and, conjecturally, if \(Y\) and \(X\) are both regular (by the absolute cohomological purity theorem, proved at least for \(X\) excellent of characteristic zero (SGA 4 XIX 3.2)). When \(X\) and \(Y\) are smooth over the field of complex numbers, one can also use the integral Chern class on the locally compact space \(X(\mathbb C)\) of complex points of \(X\). Formula (3.1) is established in this case by Atiyah and Hirzebruch [1], who place themselves more generally in the case of an immersion of complex analytic manifolds (working with the "naive" \(K'\)); it follows that the ℓ-adic formula (3.1) is valid whenever \(Y\) and \(X\) are smooth over a field which is algebraically closed and of characteristic zero, using the "Lefschetz principle" and the general results of SGA 4. This therefore makes the validity of the cohomological formula (3.1) extremely plausible; but it will be noted that it is not proved at present, even if \(X\) and \(Y\) are smooth over a field \(k\) of characteristic zero \(k\), when \(k\) is not algebraically closed.

3.4. One can also work with the cohomological theory of Chern classes, De Rham style or Hodge style [13]. The problem of the validity of (3.1) in this case arises only if \(X\) is not of characteristic zero, since one knows in any case (no. 6) that the formula in question is true modulo torsion. For smooth quasi-projective schemes over a field \(k\), formula (3.1) in its De Rham or Hodge version would moreover be a consequence of formula (3.1) in its version of cycle classes mod. algebraic equivalence, so that the De Rham or Hodge cohomological version gives another accessible way of testing the validity of (3.1) in its "cycle classes" version.

3.5. The proof [1] of the transcendental cohomological version of (3.1) is of an essentially transcendental nature, making use of tubular neighbourhoods of \(Y\) in \(X\). This suggests that the proof of a formula of type (3.1) could be linked to the systematic consideration of groups \(K'\) and of cohomology groups with support in \(Y\), a consideration which could be a sufficient technical means of "localization around \(Y\)".

footnotes.html: The formula, as well as the variant indicated in 3.3, has since been proved by P. JOUANOLOU (Nov. 1969). Cf.

pages.title: 4. Relations between \(K'(X)\) and the Chow ring \(A(X)\)

blocks.html: 4. Relations between \(K'(X)\) and the Chow ring \(A(X)\)
4.1. Suppose for simplicity that \(X\) is a smooth quasi-projective scheme over a field \(k\) (cf. no. 8 for a more general case), so that we have at our dis-

posal the Chow ring \(A(X)\) of cycle classes up to rational equivalence [4]. As variants of this ring, which is moreover defined under considerably more general conditions, we have the rings \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) (graded ring associated to the λ-filtration of \(K'(X)\)) and \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) (graded ring associated to the filtration of \(K'(X)\simeq K_\bullet(X)\) by the codimension of supports). We have theories of Chern classes with values in each of these three rings (cf. [9] for the case of \(A(X)\), and this seminar for \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\), which maps into \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\)). We also have a cohomological theory (ℓ-adic, say, where ℓ is prime to the characteristic of \(k\)) of Chern classes (SGA 5 VIII):
\[c_i(E)\in H^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\]

(where \(E\) is a vector bundle on \(X\), or again an arbitrary element of \(K'(X)\)). These cohomological theories are related by means of the canonical homomorphisms (compatible with the multiplicative structures)
\[(4.1)\qquad \xymatrix{&H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\\A(X)\ar[ur]^{\chi}\ar[rr]_{\phi}&&{\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)}&{\operatorname{Gr}'(X)}\ar[l]^{j}}\]
where we have set
\[H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))=\coprod_i H^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(i)).\]

The homomorphism \(j\) comes from the fact that the λ-filtration of \(K'(X)\) is finer than the topological filtration by the codimension of the support (Exp. X), and it is an isomorphism modulo torsion (Exp. VII 4.11). The homomorphism φ is defined for example in [RRR], and associates to the class of the codimension (i) cycle associated to a closed subscheme \(Y\) of \(X\) of codimension (i), the element \(c\ell(\mathcal O_Y)\) of \(\operatorname{Filt}^i_{\mathrm{top}}(K'(X))\bmod\operatorname{Filt}^{i+1}_{\mathrm{top}}(K'(X))\). It is a surjective homomorphism, of which we shall see further (4.2) that it is an isomorphism modulo torsion. Thus, modulo torsion the three rings \(A(X)\), \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) are canonically isomorphic, so a posteriori the Riemann-Roch formulas expressed by means of one or another of these rings (tensored with \(\mathbb Q\)) are equivalent. The graded ring \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\), used in this seminar above all as a substitute for the Chow ring, has the appreciable advantage of being defined for every locally ringed topos, and in particular for every scheme

(regular or not). On the other hand it has the disadvantage (4.6), compared with \(A(X)\), \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\), of not having as it stands a covariant character with respect to proper morphisms (even for smooth quasi-projective schemes over a field); it acquires this character only after tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\). Another advantage of \(A(X)\) over its competitors \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) is that we have a homomorphism from \(A(X)\) into \(H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\), making it possible to interpret the cohomological Chern classes as images of the Chern classes with values in \(A(X)\). This homomorphism in general does not factor through \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) (4.7), and it is doubtful, to say the least, that there exists in general a homomorphism from \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) into \(H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\) which transforms Chern classes into Chern classes (cf. no. 5). Of course, these difficulties disappear when one tensors with \(\mathbb Q\), and we find a homomorphism
\[(4.2)\qquad\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\longrightarrow H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(*))=H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\]
which transforms Chern classes into Chern classes.
4.2. To establish that the surjective homomorphism φ of (4.1) is an isomorphism modulo torsion, we use the Chern homomorphism (deduced from the theory of Chern classes [9])
\[K'(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}(A(X))\]

(where the right-hand side is the Chern ring of \(A(X)\) defined in Exp. V), which is compatible, as is easily seen, with the topological filtration of \(K'(X)\) and the natural filtration of \(\operatorname{Ch}(A(X))\), and gives, by passage to the associated graded objects, a homomorphism of graded groups
\[(4.3)\qquad\psi:\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\longrightarrow A(X).\]
This being so, we shall prove the formulas

\[(4.4)\qquad\left\{\begin{aligned}\psi\phi(x)&=(-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!\,x&&\bmod\text{ torsion pour }x\in A^i(X),\\\phi\psi(x)&=(-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!\,x&&\bmod\text{ torsion pour }x\in\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}^{\prime i}(X),\end{aligned}\right.\]

of which the first moreover implies the second, thanks to the fact that φ is surjective. These formulas evidently imply that φ and ψ are isomorphisms modulo torsion, and that up to a factor \((-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!\) in degree (i), they are inverses of each other.
To prove the first formula (4.4), we may evidently assume that \(x\) is defined by an irreducible closed subscheme \(Y\) of \(X\) of codimension (i), in which case the formula is equivalent to
\[(4.5)\qquad c_i(\mathcal O_Y)=(-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!\,c\ell(Y)\quad\bmod\text{ torsion},\]

where in the first member \(\mathcal O_Y\) is considered as a coherent sheaf on \(X\), thus defining an element of \(K'(X)\), and in the second member \(c\ell(Y)\) denotes the class of \(Y\) in \(A^i(X)\). As \(A^i(X)\) does not change when we remove from \(X\) a closed subset of codimension (>i), we are reduced to the case where \(Y\) is regular, hence smooth over the base field \(k\) if the latter is perfect. But in this last case, formula (4.5) is none other than the Riemann-Roch formula for the inclusion \(Y\longrightarrow X\) and the element 1 of \(K'(Y)\), the formula being written using the theory of Chern classes with values in the Chow ring \(A(X)\). This formula is established in [2] under the conditions envisaged here, by a method very close to the one used in this seminar for the analogous formula using \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\). This therefore establishes, at least for \(k\) perfect, formula (4.4) and consequently the fact that φ is an isomorphism mod. torsion (which in turn implies, a posteriori, that the Riemann-Roch formula "with denominators", for a proper morphism of algebraic schemes smooth over \(k\), is essentially the same, whether one writes it using \(A(X)\) as in [2], or using \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) as in this seminar!). The case where no hypothesis is made on \(k\) is easily reduced to the case where \(k\) is algebraically closed, using the fact that for a finite extension \(k'\) of degree \(n\) of \(k\), the kernel of the natural map \(A(X)\longrightarrow A(X\otimes_k k')\) is killed by \(n\) (as one sees by using the trace homomorphism
\[f_*:A(X\otimes_k k')\longrightarrow A(X),\quad\text{satisfaisant }f_*f^*=n\,\mathrm{id}).\]

4.3. It seems rather plausible that the formulas (4.4) are true as they stand and not only modulo torsion, or what amounts to the same thing, that formula 4.5 is true not only modulo torsion. This question is a very particular case of that of the validity in \(A(X)\) of the "Riemann-Roch formula without denominators" (3.1), for an immersion \(Y\longrightarrow X\), (namely the case where \(i=d\)). One may consider the validity of this particular case as rather plausible. Indeed, we have already pointed out in no. 3 that when \(k\) is of characteristic zero, the Riemann-Roch formula without denominators is valid at least in \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\), which implies in this case that both sides of (4.5) have the same image in \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\), or what amounts to the same thing, that the second of the formulas (4.4) is valid; this result will extend of itself to the case of arbitrary characteristic, once

the question raised in no. 1 has been resolved in a satisfactory way. As support for formula (4.5) in \(A(X)\) itself, let us point out the fact that it is clear a priori that the restriction of the first member to (X-Y) is zero, hence, \(Y\) being assumed irreducible, the first member must a priori be an integer multiple of \(c\ell(Y)\):
\[c_i(Y)=n(Y)c\ell(Y)\]

This already proves the exact formula (4.5) when \(c\ell(Y)\) is not a torsion element of \(A^i(X)\). It is immediate that this is the case if \(X\) is projective (and not only quasi-projective) over the field \(k\), noting that if \(ξ\in A^1(X)\) is a polarization of \(X\), then \(deg(Y·ξ^i)>0\); this proves the desired formula in this case. It follows that this formula is again valid whenever \(X\) is isomorphic to an open subscheme of a projective scheme smooth over \(k\), which will be the case when one has at one's disposal a suitable solution of the problem of resolution of singularities. This is so in particular if \(k\) is of characteristic zero, thanks to Hironaka [11].

The validity of the exact formula (4.5), hence of (4.4), would imply that the kernel of \(φ^i:A^i(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}^{\prime i}(X)\) is killed by \((i-1)!\), and in particular that \(φ^2:A^2(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}^{\prime2}(X)\) is an isomorphism. (That \(φ^1\) is an isomorphism already follows immediately from X 5.3.2). Unless I am mistaken, the fact that \(φ^2\) is an isomorphism is not proved at present, even in the particular case where \(dim X=3\). In any case, it was hastily that the writer had asserted the validity of (4.4) and (4.5) in [9, n°4, 3°].

4.4. At present, the main advantage of \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) (or of \(Gr(X)\)) over the Chow ring \(A(X)\) is the fact that it lends itself better to explicit computations of certain intersection formulas between cycles, in the case of "excess" intersections, i.e. of too large a dimension. The definition of the intersection class, with the help of Chow's lemma allowing one to move a cycle sufficiently within its class, indeed lends itself awkwardly to computation. A striking example in this regard is the following fundamental formula [Exp. VII 2.7], for a closed immersion \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) of quasi-projective schemes smooth over \(k\):
\[(4.6)\qquad f^*f_*(y)=y\,c_d(N),\]
a formula valid in \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}^*(X)\), \(y\) being in \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}^*(Y)\), \(N\) the conormal sheaf

of \(Y\) in \(X\), assumed everywhere of rank \(d\). In loc. cit., this formula follows from a very simple computation of Tor sheaves. Thanks to what we saw in 4.2, the preceding formula implies the same formula in \(A(Y)\), but only modulo torsion. It is rather extraordinary that formula (4.6) in \(A(Y)\) itself is not proved at present (*), nor even the particular case obtained by taking \(y=1\), giving the self-intersection of \(Y\):
\[(4.7)\qquad f^*(c\ell(Y))=c_d(N),\]
even if \(k\) is the field of complex numbers!

One runs into an analogous problem when one tries to compute the Chow ring of the blown-up variety \(X'\) of \(X\) along \(Y\), by modelling on the method followed for the computation of \(Gr'(X')_{\mathbb Q}\) [Exp. VII 4.8], which also works (without tensoring with (ℚ)) for the computation of \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}'(X')\). The key formula for this determination (with the notations of loc. cit.) is
\[(4.8)\qquad f^*(i_*(y))=j_*\bigl(g^*(y)c_{d-1}(\check E)\bigr),\]

a formula established at present in \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}'(X')\) (for \(y\in\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}'(Y)\)) thanks to a computation of Tor (Exp. VII 3.4), and whose validity in \(A(X')\) (for \(y\in A(Y)\)) would remain to be established.

Let us point out that even the \(ℓ\)-adic analogues of formulas (4.6), (4.7), (4.8) are not proved at present; when \(y\) is the cohomology class of an algebraic cycle, (4.6) and (4.8) are in any case true mod. torsion.

4.5. In this section and the following ones, we shall give examples where in diagram (4.1) the homomorphism \(j\), respectively the homomorphism \(φ\), is not an isomorphism. It evidently amounts to the same, for a given \(X\), to say that
\[j:\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}'(X)\]

is an isomorphism, or a monomorphism, or an epimorphism. Moreover the image of \(j\) is the subring of \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}'(X)\) generated by the Chern classes of the locally free sheaves on \(X\). In the case where \(K'(X)\) is generated as an algebra over (ℤ) by the classes of the invertible sheaves on \(X\), one sees at once that the subring of \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}'(X)\) under consideration is also the subring generated by \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}^1(X)\), the degree 1 component of \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}'(X)\).
Now let us take for \(X\) a flag variety
\[X=G/B,\]

where \(G\) is a simply connected semi-simple algebraic group over the algebraically closed field \(k\), and \(B\) a Borel subgroup [5]. When \(k=\mathbb C\), then the Bruhat cell decomposition of \(X\) [5, Exp. 13] implies that the canonical homomorphism
\[(*)\qquad K'(X)\longrightarrow K(X(\mathbb C))\]
is an isomorphism, as is moreover the canonical homomorphism
\[(**)\qquad A(X)\longrightarrow H^*(X(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z),\]

where \(X(\mathbb C)\) is the compact space of complex points of \(X\), equipped with its usual topology deduced from the topology of (ℂ). On the other hand, it was proved by L. HODGKIN [12] that \(K(X(\mathbb C))\) is indeed generated by the classes of the invertible sheaves on \(X\). The same therefore holds for \(K'(X)\) itself, and it is moreover not difficult to deduce from this, by a specialization technique using the split semi-simple groups over (ℤ) (SGA 3), that the same result remains valid for every algebraically closed field \(k\). Moreover, the cell decomposition of \(X\) (or the bijectivity of (**) in the case \(k=\mathbb C\)) easily implies that \(A(X)\) is torsion-free, hence thanks to 4.2 the homomorphism \(φ:A(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}'(X)\) is an isomorphism. From these facts it follows that \(j\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(A(X)\) is generated by its elements of degree 1, or again, if \(k=\mathbb C\), if and only if the integral cohomology of \(X(\mathbb C)\) is generated by the elements of degree 2, i.e. if the classifying space \(B_{G(\mathbb C)}\) is torsion-free. It is known on the other hand [15, p. 217 th. B (1)⇔(2), and 2.5] that this is the case if and only if \(G\) is "torsion-free", or again if \(G\) is isomorphic to a product of groups \(Sl(n)\) and \(Sp(m)\). It therefore suffices to take for \(G\) a group \(SO(n)\) with \(n\geq7\), or an exceptional simple group, to have an example where \(j\) is not an isomorphism.

4.6. It will be noted that in this example, there is no doubt that the "good" ring is the torsion-free ring \(A(X)\simeq\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}'(X)\), having as basis the cells of the Bruhat decomposition, a ring also isomorphic to the integral cohomology of \(X(\mathbb C)\) if \(k=\mathbb C\); on the other hand, the ring \(Gr'(X)\) appears as a ring having torsion of a rather pathological nature. This example therefore confirms the conception according to which the invariant \(Gr'(X)\) is hardly

interesting except modulo torsion, i.e. tensored with (ℚ). One sees at the same time, with the help of this example, that it is only when tensored with (ℚ) that \(Gr'(X)\) has a covariant character with respect to proper morphisms, even if one restricts to closed immersions of quasi-projective and smooth schemes. Indeed, if \(X\) is such that \(j\) is not an isomorphism, there exists in \(X\) an integral closed subscheme \(Y\) such that \(c\ell'(Y)\in K'(X)\) is not in \(Filt^i(X)\), where \(i=\operatorname{codim}(Y,X)\). We may take \(Y\) of minimal dimension, so that \(Filt^{i+1}(X)=\operatorname{Filt}_{\mathrm{top}}^{i+1}(X)\). If \(Z\) is the singular set of \(Y\), and \(X'=X-Z\), \(Y'=Y-Z\), one concludes that \(c\ell'(Y')\in K'(X')\) is not in \(Filt^i(X')\) (taking into account that \(K'(X)\longrightarrow K'(X')\) is surjective and compatible with the augmentations, and that its kernel is \(Im(K'(Z)\longrightarrow K'(X))\subset\operatorname{Filt}_{\mathrm{top}}^{i+1}(X)=\operatorname{Filt}^{i+1}(X)\)). Let us then consider the inclusion
\[f:Y'\longrightarrow X',\]
which is a closed immersion of codimension (i) of quasi-projective and smooth schemes over \(k\), such that
\[f_*:K'(Y')\longrightarrow K'(X')\]
is not compatible with the \(λ\)-filtrations (modulo a shift by (i)), since \(f_*(1)\) is not in \(Filt^i(X')\).
4.7. Keeping the notations of 4.5, let us now note that it follows from (Exp. IX) that the cited theorem of HODGKIN is equivalent to the fact that we have an isomorphism
\[K'(G)\simeq\mathbb Z,\quad\text{donc }\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}'(G)\simeq\mathbb Z,\]

the homomorphism \(K'(G/B)\longrightarrow K'(G)\) being a priori surjective and having as kernel the ideal generated by the \(c\ell'(L)-1\), where \(L\) runs over the invertible Modules on \(X=G/B\) associated to the representations \(B\longrightarrow G_m\) (which in fact give all the invertible modules on \(X\) [5, Exp. 15]). One sees on the other hand by an analogous argument that
\[A(G/B)\longrightarrow A(G)\]

is also surjective, and that its kernel is the ideal generated by the elements of \(A^1(G/B)\) defined by the representations \(B\longrightarrow G_m\), hence in fact by all of \(A^1(G/B)\).(*) This shows that \(A(G)\) is reduced to \(A^*(X)=\mathbb Z\) if and
only if "\(G\) is torsion-free", i.e. \(G\) is isomorphic to a product of groups \(Sl(n)\) and \(Sp(m)\). Hence in this case, and in this case only, the homomorphism
\[\phi:A(G)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Gr}_{\mathrm{top}}'(G)\]
is an isomorphism. Hence if \(G\) has torsion, the underlying scheme provides an example of a non-bijective \(φ\).
4.8. One finds likewise an example where the homomorphism \(γ\) of (4.1)
\[(4.9)\qquad\gamma:A(G)\longrightarrow H^{2*}(G,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\]

does not factor through \(Gr_{\mathrm{top}}'(G)\), or what amounts to the same thing here, an example where this homomorphism is not zero in degrees > 0. Restricting (as is permissible thanks to suitable specialization theorems) to the case where \(k=\mathbb C\), we are reduced to studying the homomorphism
\[(4.10)\qquad A(G)\longrightarrow H^*(G(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z),\]
deduced moreover by passage to the quotient from the canonical homomorphism
\[A(G/B)\simeq H^*(G/B(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z)\longrightarrow H^*(G(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z).\]

It is tempting to conjecture that the homomorphism (4.10) is in fact injective, since one knows moreover that the torsion primes for \(G(\mathbb C)\) are exactly those dividing the order of the finite group \(A^+(G)=\coprod_{i>0}A^i(G)\). If this were so, as soon as \(G\) had torsion, the homomorphism (4.10) would not factor through \(φ\), hence for suitable \(ℓ\) (precisely, for \(ℓ\) dividing the order of \(A^+(G)\), i.e. \(ℓ\) a torsion coefficient of \(G\)) the same would hold for (4.9). One can at least affirm that in the case of the group \(G=G_2\) over the field (ℂ), the homomorphism (4.10) is not zero in degree > 0; precisely, there exists an element of \(A^3(G)\) whose image in \(H^6(G(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z)\) is of order 2; it therefore follows that the homomorphism (4.9) does not factor through \(φ\) when \(ℓ=2\). The fact used concerning \(G_2\), which amounts to saying that there exists in \(H^6(G/B(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z)\) an element whose image in \(H^6(G(\mathbb C),\mathbb Z)\) is non-zero, is found in [3], modulo the well-known dictionary between the language of complex semi-simple algebraic groups and that of compact Lie groups; it was kindly communicated to me by J.P. SERRE.

footnotes.html: It is therefore established, thanks to P. JOUANOLOU, for \(X\) smooth quasi-projective over a field (cf. note p. 5).

blocks.html: 5. Relations between \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) and \(H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\)
5.1. The example given in 4.8 does not exclude that one might perhaps be able to find, for an arbitrary scheme \(X\), a ring homomorphism
\[g':\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\longrightarrow H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*)),\]

such that for every locally free Module \(E\) on \(X\), \(g'^i\) transforms the Chern class \(c_i(E)\) of Exp. V into the cohomological Chern class of SGA 5 VII. Of course, here \(\ell\) denotes a prime number distinct from the residue characteristics of \(X\). As the ring \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) is generated by the \(c^i(E)\), one sees in any case that \(g'\) is uniquely determined by the condition of being compatible with the Chern classes.
5.2. Using the Chern homomorphism
\[K'(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}(H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))),\]

we deduce from it, by passage to the graded objects associated respectively to the filtration of the first member and the filtration by degree of the second, a homomorphism of graded groups (but not of rings!)
\[\psi:\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\longrightarrow H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*)),\]
which satisfies the relation
\[\psi(c_i(E))=(-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!\,c_i^\ell(E),\]
where \(c_i^\ell(E)\) denotes the \(\ell\)-adic Chern class. We deduce from it, by division starting from the \(\psi^i\), a ring homomorphism
\[g_{\mathbb Q}:\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\longrightarrow H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(*))=H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell,\]

transforming Chern classes into Chern classes; and such that the image of the first member is contained in that of \(H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\); consequently, the question of the existence of \(g\) is resolved affirmatively provided that we replace \(H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\) by its quotient by its torsion subgroup. The first doubtful case for defining the components \(g^i\) of the hypothetical homomorphism \(g\) is that of \(g^3\); a priori one only manages to define \(2g^3=\psi^3\). The answer to the question of the existence of \(g\) would be negative if for example one could find three invertible sheaves \(L_1,L_2,L_3\) on \(X\) satisfying the relation
\[(L_1-1)(L_2-1)(L_3-1)=0\quad\text{dans }K'(X),\]
but such that we have
\[c_1(L_1)c_1(L_2)c_1(L_3)\ne0\quad\text{dans }H^6(X,\mu_2^{\otimes3}).\]
5.3. When \(X\) is smooth and quasi-projective over a field \(k\), one can likewise raise the question of the existence of a ring homomorphism
\[g:\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\longrightarrow A(X)\]

with values in the Chow ring \(A(X)\), transforming Chern classes into Chern classes, and one can repeat in this connection the preceding reflections, which show in particular how to construct \((-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!g^i\) directly in the absence of \(g^i\) itself.

5.4. Let us point out, to conclude this section, that essentially the same question arises if, instead of a scheme \(X\), we consider a compact (say) topological space \(X\), which again makes it possible to consider the graded object \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\) of \(K'(X)\) equipped with its \(\lambda\)-filtration, and to set oneself the task of finding a ring homomorphism
\[\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\longrightarrow H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z)\]
transforming Chern classes into Chern classes.

pages.title: 6. Cohomological Riemann-Roch theorem, and Gysin homomorphism

blocks.html: 6. Cohomological Riemann-Roch theorem, and Gysin homomorphism

6.1. For simplicity, we shall restrict ourselves here to schemes \(X\) admitting an ample invertible Module, which implies that one does not have to distinguish between the naive \(K'(X)\) and the other one. Let us begin with the Riemann-Roch formula "with denominators", for a proper complete intersection morphism \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\). As (for a prime number \(\ell\) prime to the residue characteristics of \(Y\)) one can define the \(\ell\)-adic Chern classes of a locally free Module \(E\),
\[c_i(E)\in H^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\]
(SGA 5 VII), corresponding to a homomorphism
\[(6.1)\qquad K'(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ch}(H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))),\]
the question arises of giving a cohomological variant of the Riemann-Roch formula. The only ingredient missing in order to give a meaning to the formula is a Gysin homomorphism
\[(6.2)\qquad H^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\longrightarrow H^{n-2d}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(i-d)),\]

where \(d\) is the virtual relative dimension of \(f\). This homomorphism being assumed defined, and taking into account the Riemann-Roch formula in the form of Exp. VIII, with values in \(\operatorname{Gr}'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}\), one finds that the validity of the \(\ell\)-adic Riemann-Roch formula for the morphism \(f\) is exactly equivalent to the commutativity of the square
\[(6.3)\qquad\xymatrix{{\operatorname{Gr}'(X)_{\mathbb Q}}\ar[r]\ar[d]&H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(*))\ar[d]\\{\operatorname{Gr}'(Y)_{\mathbb Q}}\ar[r]&H^{2*}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(*)),}\]

where the horizontal arrows are defined from (6.1) by passing to the associated graded objects and dividing, so as to transform Chern classes into Chern classes, and where the vertical arrows are respectively the arrow of Exp. VIII and the Gysin homomorphism in cohomology (assumed given).

6.2. Let us note that the Gysin homomorphism is defined in any case if \(Y\) is smooth over a field \(k\) (SGA 5 IV), using the relative cohomological purity theorem over \(k\); the same construction applies when \(Y\) is excellent regular and of characteristic zero, thanks to the absolute cohomological purity theorem of SGA 4 XIX, and it will apply automatically for every excellent and regular \(Y\), without restriction on the characteristic, once the absolute cohomological purity theorem is proved in full generality. Finally, the same construction applies, thanks to the relative cohomological purity theorem (SGA 4 XVI 3.7), when \(X\) and \(Y\) are both smooth over one and the same scheme \(S\) and \(f\) is a \(S\)-morphism. There is reason to conjecture that in each of these cases where the Gysin homomorphism is defined, the diagram (6.3) is indeed commutative. The only case where this assertion is verified at present is that where \(X\) and \(Y\) are both smooth over a field \(k\), and it then follows from the commutativity of the diagram analogous to (6.3):
\[(6.4)\qquad\xymatrix{A(X)\ar[r]\ar[d]&H^{2*}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(*))\ar[d]\\A(Y)\ar[r]&H^{2*}(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell(*)),}\]
involving the rings of cycle classes (mod. rational equivalence) \(A(X)\) and \(A(Y)\), a commutativity established in SGA 5 IV.

6.3. Let us note that in each of the cases envisaged above where one manages to define a Gysin homomorphism, one obtains in fact a construction for cohomology with coefficients in \(\mathbb Z_\ell(*)\), and not only in \(\mathbb Q_\ell(*)\). This makes it possible, when \(f\) is an immersion, to raise the question of the validity of the Riemann-Roch formula "without denominators", as explained in no. 3. When \(X\) and \(Y\) are both smooth over a field \(k\), the commutativity of (6.4) shows us that it would suffice to prove the formula without denominators in the Chow ring \(A(Y)\) cf. (3.3).

6.4. When no regularity hypothesis is made on \(Y\), and even if \(X\) and \(Y\) are of finite type over the field \(\mathbb C\), one no longer sees any plausible definition of a cohomological Gysin homomorphism (6.2), even for a regular immersion of codimension 1. Can one nevertheless find one with reasonable properties, in particular making the square (6.3) commutative? The question is evidently linked to that of defining, for a complete intersection \(X\) of codimension \(i\) in \(Y\), "its cohomology class"
\[\gamma(X)\in H^{2i}(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell(i)),\]

so as to transform intersections (without excess components) into cup-products. This last question is resolved affirmatively at least modulo torsion, i.e. in \(H^{2*}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(*))\), by defining
\[\gamma(X)=1/(-1)^{i-1}(i-1)!\,c_i(\mathcal O_X).\]

But I do not know whether \(c_i(\mathcal O_X)\) is divisible by \((i-1)!\) in \(H^{2i}(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\). In particular, if \(Y\) is a projective scheme of dimension 3 over the field of complex numbers, say, and \(X\) is reduced to a (singular) point of \(Y\), is \(c_3(\mathcal O_X)\) divisible by 2 in \(H^6(Y,\mathbb Z_2(3))\), and in particular is the Chern class zero in \(H^6(Y,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)\)? Let us also point out, in the same vein, the following question: let \(Y\) be a (singular) projective scheme of dimension 4, \(X\) a closed subscheme of dimension 1 which is globally an intersection of three positive divisors \(D_1,D_2,D_3\); consider the product of their Chern classes mod. 2:
\[c_1(D_1)c_1(D_2)c_1(D_3)\in H^6(Y,\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z);\]
is this element of the \(H^6\) independent of the particular choice of the \(D_i\) with intersection \(X\)?
7. Chern classes of perfect complexes

7.1. When one raises the question of developing a cohomological Riemann-Roch formula, with or without denominators, outside the rather artificial hypothesis of the existence of an ample invertible Module on the schemes under consideration, one is necessarily confronted with the question of a definition, for a perfect complex of Modules \(L.\) on a scheme \(X\), of the Chern classes
\[c_i(L.)\in H^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(i)),\]

generalizing the Chern classes of locally free Modules SGA 5 VII. The same question arises moreover for general locally ringed topoi (compare [10]), as well as for perfect complexes on paracompact spaces ringed by the continuous complex functions, working then with ordinary integral cohomology. In the case of a compact space, one knows that the \(K'(X)\) constructed with perfect complexes is the same as the "naive" one constructed with locally free Modules (Exp. IV), and in this case one is trivially reduced to the classical definition. But already in the case of a locally compact space the problem posed seems non-trivial, since the integral cohomology of such a space is not always the projective limit of the cohomologies of its compact subsets.

7.2. More precisely, when \(Y\) is a closed subset of \(X\) such that \(L.|(X-Y)\) is zero (in the sense of derived categories), i.e. such that the cohomology sheaves of \(L.\) have supports in \(Y\), one would like to define Chern classes with support in \(Y\)
\[c_i^Y(L.)\in H_Y^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\quad\text{resp.}\quad H_Y^{2i}(X,\mathbb Z),\]
which would give back the "ordinary" (sic) Chern classes of 7.1 by means of the canonical homomorphism
\[H_Y^*(X,-)\longrightarrow H^*(X,-).\]

Even in the case where the latter reduce to the Chern classes of locally free Modules (\(X\) a scheme with an ample invertible Module, or a compact space), the construction of these Chern classes "with supports" has not been carried out, and doubtless will only be possible at the same time as that of the general Chern classes postulated in 7.1.(*)

It is probable that the Chern classes with supports will have a role to play in the proof of the cohomological Riemann-Roch formula without denominators (cf. 3.5). On the other hand, they should make it possible to give an improved version of the Riemann-Roch formula (with or without denominators) by introducing families of supports into this formula.
8. Chow ring of regular schemes

The problem is to define rational equivalence of cycles, and to study the ring of classes obtained (in particular its functorial properties), for a regular noetherian scheme \(X\) admitting an ample invertible Module, generalizing the theory known when \(X\) is smooth over a field [4]. The key technical result will again be a "moving lemma", ensuring that one can vary a cycle within its class so as to intersect a given cycle in the right dimension.

The problem envisaged is rather natural a priori, all the more so as we have seen (4.5 to 4.8) that the substitutes \(\operatorname{Gr}'(X)\), \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\) for the Chow ring \(A(X)\) are hardly satisfactory when one does not restrict oneself to a theory "modulo torsion". When \(X\) is deduced from a projective and smooth algebraic scheme \(Y\) over a field \(k\), by taking the local scheme at the vertex of a projecting cone \(C\) and removing the origin, one finds (granting that \(A(X)\) is indeed the inductive limit of the Chow rings of the \(U-\{O\}\), where \(U\) runs over the open neighbourhoods in \(C\) of the vertex \(O\)) that \(A(X)\) is canonically isomorphic to the quotient ring \(A(Y)/\xi A(Y)\), where \(\xi\in A^1(Y)\) is induced by the projective immersion under consideration of \(Y\). This shows that the knowledge of the Chow ring \(A(X)\) (of non-classical type) is equivalent to the knowledge of \(A(Y)/\xi A(Y)\), which one could call the "primitive part" of the Chow ring \(A(Y)\) (in a sense suggested by the Lefschetz-Hodge cohomological theory of non-singular projective algebraic varieties). This example shows that the "local Chow rings" of local rings with an isolated
singularity are entirely non-trivial geometric invariants, and ones which will doubtless have their role to play in the "arithmetic" study of these local rings.

Of course, once in possession of a reasonable notion of Chow ring for a regular noetherian scheme admitting an ample invertible Module, there is reason to generalize to this case the relations established resp. conjectured in no. 4 between this ring and the ring \(\operatorname{Gr}'_{\mathrm{top}}(X)\), and to formulate in terms of this ring the question of the "Riemann-Roch formula without denominators" stated in no. 3.

footnotes.html: Added in October 1968. The problems raised here have been solved in the framework of <u>Hodge cohomology</u> by L. ILLUSIE; cf. [13]

blocks.html: BIBLIOGRAPHY

[1] M.F. Atiyah and F. Hirzebruch, The Riemann-Roch theorem for analytic embeddings, Topology, vol. 1, p. 151-166 (1962).

[2] A. Borel and J.P. Serre, Le théorème de Riemann-Roch, Bull. Soc. Math. France, vol. 86, p. 97-136 (1958).

[3] R. Bott and H. Samelson, Amer. Journ. of Math. vol. 80, p. 1004 (1958).

[4] C. Chevalley, Les classes d’équivalence rationnelle, I et II, Séminaire à l’ENS 1958, exposés 2 and 3.

[5] C. Chevalley, Classification des groupes de Lie algébriques, Séminaire à l’ENS 1956/58.

[6] A. Dold and D. Puppe, Homologie nicht-additiver Funktoren. Anwendungen, Annales de l’Inst. Fourier t. XI, p. 201-312 (1961).

[7] H. Grauert, Ein Theorem der analytischen Garbentheorie, Pub. Math. n° 5, p. 5-64 (1960).

[8] A. Grothendieck, Classes de faisceaux et théorème de Riemann-Roch, mimeographed notes prepared by the Institute for Advanced Study, 1957 (work cited [RRR] in the present exposé, and which is reproduced in our seminar in the form of an Appendix to Chap. 0).

[9] A. Grothendieck, La théorie des classes de Chern, Bull. Soc. Math. France vol. 86, p. 137-154 (1958).

[10] A. Grothendieck, Classes de Chern et représentations linéaires des groupes discrets, in: Dix exposés sur la cohomologie des schémas, Masson-North Holland Pub. Cie.

[11] H. Hironaka, Resolution of singularities of an algebraic variety over a field of characteristic zero, Ann. of Math. 79, p. 109-326 (1964).

[12] L. Hodgkin, thesis (to appear?).

[13] L. Illusie, work in preparation.

[14] J.P. Serre, Algèbre Locale, Multiplicités, Lecture Notes in Math. n° 11 (1965) Springer.

[15] A. Borel, Sous-groupes commutatifs et torsion des groupes de Lie compacts connexes, Tohoku Math. Journal, vol. 13, p. 216-240 (1961).

[16] (*) P. Deligne, Exp. XVIII in Théorie des topos et cohomologie étale des schémas (SGA 6), by M. Artin, A. Grothendieck and J.L. Verdier.

[17] (*) U. Mausín, Correspondances, motifs et transformations monoïdales (in Russian). MAt. Sborik, t 77 (119) n° 4, 1968.
Note for page 1

The theory of the operations \(\lambda^i\) in \(D^-(X)\), and of the operations \(\lambda^i\) in \(K^\bullet(X)\), was written up by P. Deligne in 1968. He proves that \(K^\bullet(X)\) thus becomes a pre-\(\lambda\)-ring, and, when \(\mathcal O_X\) is a sheaf of Algebras over a field, that \(K^\bullet(X)\) is even a \(\lambda\)-ring. It is plausible that a refinement of Deligne's method would make it possible to get rid of the restrictive hypothesis on \(\mathcal O_X\).
Notes for page 11

(*) (April 1969) D. MUMFORD informs us of a proof of (9.6) in the Chow ring (without neglecting torsion), which he obtained in a seminar at Harvard in 1959! The reader will find the proof of this beautiful result in SGA 5 VII 9. This result evidently contains the corresponding cohomological result, pointed out in note (**). However, the cohomological result is proved by J. P. JOUANOLOU under more general conditions (without a quasi-projective hyperplane, and in the relative smooth case over an arbitrary base).

(**) (January 1969) J.P. JOUANOLOU has just proved these formulas for every \(y\) without neglecting torsion, cf. SGA 5 VII § 4.9. DELIGNE and MANIN [7] had previously remarked independently, and by (easy) arguments of a rather different nature, that it sufficed to establish the formulas for \(y\) "algebraic", and even for \(y=1\) in 16, in order to conclude the same relation in the general case.
